# Quick guide for AI coding agents working on PhyloZoo

Purpose
- Fast orientation for automated coding agents contributing small, well-tested edits to PhyloZoo.

Big-picture architecture (read these dirs/files to understand the "why")
- `src/phylozoo/`: core package
  - `core/` (networks, distances, primitives), `inference/` (squirrel algorithms), `viz/` (plotting)
  - Key files: `src/phylozoo/core/network/sdnetwork/sd_phynetwork.py`, `src/phylozoo/core/distance/base.py`
- `tests/`: pytest suite (see `tests/conftest.py` for fixtures & `src` path handling).
- `docs/` and `examples/`: canonical usage and manual pages.

Quick commands
- Dev install: `pip install -e .` or `pip install -e "[dev]"` (recommended for development)
- Run tests: `pytest` (project configured via `pyproject.toml` addopts: `-v --strict-markers --tb=short`)
- Type check: `mypy src/phylozoo`
- Format & lint: `black src/phylozoo tests` and `ruff check src/phylozoo tests`
- Build docs: `make -C docs html` or `docs/autobuild.sh`

Project-specific conventions (follow these exactly)
- ALWAYS use full type hints (incl. `__init__`, `__repr__`); prefer built-in generics (e.g. `list[int]`, `int | None`).
- ALWAYS use NumPy-style docstrings; any example in a docstring must be mirrored in tests.
- Tests: use pytest naming (`test_*.py`, `test_*` functions), put tests in `tests/`, include docstrings in tests. Prefer `SemiDirectedPhyNetwork` fixtures unless testing `MixedPhyNetwork` specifically.
- Performance: use `numba` only when it measurably helps; add micro-benchmarks and record experiments in `sandbox/findings.txt`.

Patterns & gotchas (concrete examples)
- DistanceMatrix: `src/phylozoo/core/distance/base.py` makes underlying numpy array read-only and stores labels as tuples—labels must be hashable.
- I/O: SemiDirectedPhyNetwork uses `IOMixin` and supports eNewick and PhyloZoo-DOT (.pzdot) formats.
- Numba: many modules are numba-aware; avoid changes that make code JIT-incompatible.
- Test path hack: `tests/conftest.py` inserts `src/` into `sys.path`; prefer editable install instead of relying on this.
- Tooling: `pyproject.toml` is authoritative for Python version (>=3.10) and tool configs.

Docs guidance (for pages like the `distance_matrices` manual page)
- Start by naming the module (e.g., `phylozoo.core.distance`) and saying "everything on this page can be imported from there".
- Describe the main goal and usage of the module in one paragraph.
- Describe the main class (e.g., `DistanceMatrix`), how to create it, and IO examples.
- Go deeper into helper functions and utilities.
- End with a short API reference linking to the autogenerated docs (use `:func:` / `:class:` or cross-reference to the API).

How to propose changes (short checklist for humans reviewing patches)
1. Make minimal, well-scoped code edits and add tests under `tests/`.
2. Run `pytest -q` and `mypy src/phylozoo` locally; fix issues introduced where possible.
3. Format (`black`) and lint (`ruff`) before submitting.
4. Provide a short, descriptive commit message and a PR description with failing/passing examples and any micro-benchmarks.

Agent rules
- Prefer code edits and unit tests over running arbitrary commands.
- NEVER push commits, create branches, or perform VCS operations on behalf of maintainers—produce diffs/patches or proposed edits for human review.
- If unsure about behavior, ask the maintainer rather than changing tests to match current behavior.

If anything here is ambiguous or you want more examples (test templates, numba checklist, docs templates), tell me which piece to expand and I will iterate.
